(She saved one of the most groan-inducing for her engagement announcement, writing: “Your English teacher and your gym teacher are getting married.”) And while it feels incongruous for Swift, a noted fan of a sourdough starter, to make “wine moms” the butt of the joke in songs, perhaps it only adds to her bona fides: there’s nothing cringier than a lack of self-awareness.Owen MyersImage credits: Instagram/taylorswift; Startraks/REX/ShutterstockShe made competency a flexView image in fullscreenWhile Swift’s songs are full of wayward emotions, she runs her career with military precision. As a kid, when her friends were watching the Disney Channel, she studied episodes of VH1’s Behind the Music and deduced that artists went off the rails when “they lost their level of self-awareness”. It made her hawkish about ambition and execution: as a teenager, she outwitted Nashville stalwarts, challenging them on industry norms and understanding exactly how to play the game to get ahead. When she was criticised for her singing or dancing, she skilled up. When foes came for her, she wised up: “I got smarter, I got harder in the nick of time,” as she sang on Look What You Made Me Do. From big-picture concepts to the finest details, Swift is a one-woman panopticon on her own career.Of course, all-encompassing professional excellence is also a very Beyoncé trait – but she doesn’t show her hand the same way. Swift wrote the 21st-century playbook on elite pop stardom, and she wants us to know it.LSImage credits: Gareth Cattermole/TAS24/Getty Images for TAS Rights ManagementShe bent formats to her willView image in fullscreenSwift understands streaming better than almost any of her contemporaries. Although she was late to warm to the format, in the 2020s she has proved herself a master of the form: deluxe editions of 2022’s Midnights and 2024’s The Tortured Poets Department, each essentially doubling their length, were released mere hours after the originals. Streaming is a volume game when it comes to breaking records; adding tracks in this way encourages repeat listens, without the threat of fans getting tired or bored. And it’s an extremely linear medium: Tortured Poets and The Life of a Showgirl each had their single placed as track one, so that it would notch up the most consumption and become the default hit.When it comes to understanding format – and how format relates to commercial dominance – Swift has long been ahead of the curve. Among pop stars, she popularised the practice of selling vinyl records with exclusive covers in order to boost chart positions, previously only the purview of superstar rappers. CD copies of her 2019 album Lover came with an exclusive notebook containing snippets of her own personal diaries – but fans had to buy four copies in order to read all the pages. (Similarly, Reputation’s CDs came with one of two exclusive magazines, and 1989’s deluxe edition came with a randomised set of Polaroid-style photos.)Swift was savvy with format early in her career, too. In seeming acknowledgment of the fact that country has a far bigger audience in the US than overseas, she bundled international editions of her 2008 breakthrough album Fearless with singles from her debut, while certain songs from 2010’s Speak Now were redone with pop mixes for the international market. She also seemingly took to heart the advertising industry adage to meet your customer where they are, selling copies of her fourth album Red at Papa John’s pizzerias.Of course, all of this paled in comparison to Taylor’s Versions, her monumental album re-recording project, which allowed her to reactivate older music in her catalogue and re-market it to new fans. The re-recorded albums also included new, previously unreleased songs, doubly incentivising existing fans to repurchase copies of albums they already owned. In the years since Swift began releasing the Taylor’s Versions, other artists in dire contract situations, such as JoJo, have followed suit, while some have simply done it for fun: Hilary Duff packaged her new album with new recordings of hits such as Come Clean. In essence, Swift turned what was once seen as a desperate business move into a shrewd marketing play.SDSImage credits: Jane Barlow/PAShe set new melodic standardsView image in fullscreenGracie Abrams, Maisie Peters and Holly Humberstone.Once you know about theT-Drop, you’ll start noticing it everywhere. The Switched on Pop podcast hosts coined this term to describe Swift’s penchant for dropping into her lower register without warning to give a sly sting in the tail to her melodies, particularly when they are already dripping with sarcasm. See her deliciously drawn-out delivery of “drunk and grumbling on about howI … can’t … sing” inMean.Her acolytes love to use the T-drop as a vehicle for their ultra-specific, highly Swiftian imagery, and drippy recent hits from Gracie Abrams, Maisie Peters and Holly Humberstone (pictured) hinge on the device. These singers get Swift’s emotion but not her knack for goofy – instantly memorable – imagery that is a little odd, or downright off: “Sexy baby”, “grinning like a devil”, hell, even “this sick beat”.OMImage credits: Ian West/PA; Roy Rochlin; Viktor Szymanowicz/Anadolu via Getty ImagesShe made game day for the girlsView image in fullscreenOn 24 September 2023, Swift attended her first ever Kansas City Chiefs game in support of her beau, star tight endTravis Kelce. “It’s like these two cultural titans – the NFL and Taylor Swift – were coming together,” says Bryan Graham, the Guardian’s deputy US sports editor. Swift was no longer on the bleachers, but firmly in the executive suite of stadiums, marking what Ella Brockway, assistant US sports editor, calls “a different level of celebrity in sports to what we’ve seen before”. Swift’s entry into the NFL world may have been due to love, but it also didn’t hurt that it came with the opportunity for personal brand expansion.OMWhat does Taylor Swift mean to theNFL?BGFor years, the NFL has been desperately courting women, back to 2009 when they started celebrating breast cancer awareness month and selling pink merchandise. So when they embraced her with a lot of cutaways to her during games, especially in the 2023-24 season, it made a lot of sense. It felt like manna from heaven for the NFL.EBThe first day [she came to a game] was the most fun. I had a lot of Swift update accounts on my timeline that were sharing things like football explainers for the confused. But the second game was when I realised that this narrative was going to change the NFL season. It was a Monday night game playing the New York Jets, and she brought Blake Lively and Ryan Reynolds. ESPN was tweeting out highlights of Swift walking through the tunnel at MetLife Stadium.BGThis is the American equivalent of the royal family. I mean, the idea of “the cheerleader and the quarterback” fits into the mythology of her music going back to You Belong With Me. Her relationship with Kelce felt like a real-life actualisation of that fantasy.Were more female fans tuning in to watch the Chiefs when Swift was at the game?BGThere was a huge lift in female viewership.EBThe NFL has had a few years of controversy with domestic violence issues among players, and [Swift] helped them to attract a female audience. Swift was wearing all this vintage Chiefs merch to the games, and then the NFL was changing all of its merch and doing partnerships with designers. So if you wanted a Chiefs shirt it could be something that twentysomething women were actually interested in wearing versus like, a Chiefs logo in pink.Swift’s romance with Kelce coincided with her reaching a new peak of superstardom withthe Eras tour.EBShe was sly enough to figure out how to own this other world at the same time and penetrate into a new market. There were still some “Brads and Chads”, as she put it, who were mad about her being there. But I think the longer she stayed around, the more it was like: “OK, this girl can hang.”Why do you think she chose Travis and Jason Kelce’s podcastNew Heights to announce The Life of a Showgirl?BGIt’s kind of a flex. It’s couple goals: lifting each other up and mutually pointing to each other’s projects. I think that was probably the easiest decision of the rollout. It’s like: “I have this property in the family already. Why would I go to someone else?”A huge part of American sports business in the last 15 years has been about athletes bypassing traditional media channels and owning their own story.Will she ever do the Super Bowl half-time show?BGWell, artists don’t get paid to perform, and they generally do it to promote a tour. And she didn’t need a lot of help selling tickets for the Eras tour. The NFL absolutely would need her more than she needed it.The Eras tour was also a showcase of her own athleticism.EBTravis has said that they were attracted to each other in part because they’re both athletes in a sense, and she talked in Time magazine about doing the whole set while running on a treadmill. I don’t think she saw herself as an athlete around the time of the Fearless tour, but that’s certainly changed as stadium shows have become part of her thing.Image Credits:Ezra Shaw, Getty ImagesShe exposed the music bizView image in fullscreenScooter Braun and Scott Borchetta.As a young pop fan in the late 90s, my knowledge of the music industry extended to: Virgin Records (the Spice Girls’ record label) and Simon Fuller (the Spice Girls’ manager). That is literally child’s play compared with the advanced industry knowhow any tween Swiftie has today thanks to her very public stance on artist rights – particularly her own.In 2014, Swift removed her back catalogue from Spotify in protest at the idea that music should be free. (She put it back in 2017.) A year later, she was credited with getting Apple Music to back down from declining to pay royalties on music listened to during its free three-month introductory trial period – although independent record labels and trade bodies had also refused to work with Apple’s terms, theyreceived no such public creditfor their lobbying.In 2018, Swift left Big Machine, the label she had signed to as a teenager, for Universal Music Group (UMG). The deal allowed her to own the rights to her master recordings; she also demanded that if UMG sold their Spotify shares, the money would be distributed to its acts on a non-recoupable basis – ie not to settle outstanding debts. (As of April, artists are benefiting from that deal.)In 2019, Big Machine sold the master recording rights to Swift’s first six albums to industry executive Scooter Braun. Swift called it her “worst case scenario”, claiming that Braun had bullied Swift alongside his clients Ye and Justin Bieber. Soon after, Swift said in a TV interview that she intended tore-record those first six albumsto reclaim ownership over them. I confess, when I interviewed her just after this, I didn’t ask her about it because it seemed like such a deranged idea. What an idiot.With note-for-note likenesses, the Taylor’s Version re-recordings were blockbuster successes, often charting higher than the original albums (despite generally sounding worse) and inspiring the billion-dollar-grossing Eras tour. They utterly torpedoed Braun’s investment – as songwriter on those master recordings, Swift could deny their use in soundtracks and insist that directors use her new versions, and encourage streaming platforms to prioritise them – and they inspired mass awareness of granular music-industry machinations. Thereafter many young female artists signedalmost unprecedented debut-album dealsthat let them own their masters, including Olivia Rodrigo, Ice Spice and Maggie Rogers; Zara Larsson bought hers off her old label to start her own.But this heightened awareness led to some fans misinterpreting the idea of labels owning master recordings as universally shady, rather than standard practice in exchange for monetary advances. It also gave some artists misaligned expectations, including Rina Sawayama, a British-Japanese pop star, who protested against her label Dirty Hit owning her masters after her label mate (and former label director)Matty Healyof the 1975 laughed as others mocked Asian accents on a 2023 podcast. His comments were widely criticised, but there was also nothing nefarious about a label owning a young artist’s music. Swift has exceptional bargaining power: most artists do not make enough money to have that sort of leverage, but may now expect it.Masters hadn’t had so much publicity since Prince protested against Warner Bros owning his in 1995. Swift created the most business-literate group of fans and artists in history, and in turn a bulwark against exploitation. Headlines once confined to music trade publications are now mainstream news. The popularisation of licensing deals means many artists have their rights revert after a certain period. And last May, Swift got her masters back: Braun had sold them to a private equity firm, which sold them to her. Revealing the news, Swift framed the saga as a kind of fateful crusade: “Every time a new artist tells me they negotiated to own their master recordings in their record contract because of this fight, I’m reminded of how important it was for all of this to happen.”LSImage Credits:Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Ithaca HoldingsShe redefined album ‘eras’View image in fullscreenSwift was hardly the first pop star to reinvent herself with each album, but she was arguably the first to turn the idea of discrete “eras” into a marketing tool. With her Eras tour, she merchandised a concept previously owned by fans and made it a pillar of her business model. Now, she sells the same cardigan in a new colour scheme alongside each new album and reissues old merch with new photos. She even revamped the Eras tour halfway through its run to include a new Era (naturally, the Disney+ film of the tour was also revamped). The concept has since been adopted by countless other artists – turning the dreaded “greatest hits” tour, once a signal an artist was over the hill culturally, into a sign of strength.SDSImage credits: Matt Sayles/AP; Beth Garrabrant; Xavi Torrent/TAS24/Getty Images for TAS Rights ManagementShe turned fans into detectivesView image in fullscreenSince her2006 debut, Swift, inspired by the (then Dixie) Chicks,left secret coded messages about her songs’ subjects in the liner notes to heralbums. It primed Swiftiesto approach her work like the Rosetta stone, and stoked the intimacy between them. From 2017’s Reputation, Swift swapped the trick from liner notes to the visual realm, peppering her videos, outfits, social media posts, tours and interviews with clues about her plans. Although articles decoding Swiftian Easter eggs are now prettymuch a cornerstone of entertainment media – the world’s biggest ongoing treasure hunt – they have still retained that sense of intimacy on a mass level. As fans whocrack the codes feelwildly validated for the depth of theirunderstanding, the endless speculation is free press out of any marketer’s wildestdreams.LSImage credits: Jeff Kravitz/FilmMagicShe rewired pop criticismView image in fullscreenIn many ways, 2015 doesn’t seem that long ago – until you think about how that year, US indie bible Pitchfork decided to review now-disgraced rock star Ryan Adams’ full-album cover of Swift’s 2014 album 1989,having not reviewed the original. The concept of poptimism – that pop is as worthy of critical consideration as rock – was a decade old, but the old snobberies were still hanging around. Swift’s crossover from country to pop made her unignorable and exposed the unfailing integrity of her songwriting to a mass audience. Emerging young female critics – such as Tavi Gevinson, the founder of Rookie magazine – knew the emotional and technical acuity of Swift’s work meant it should be treated like scripture. As her star rose and her fanbase became more vociferous, jaundiced critics knew they had to take Swift seriously if they wanted to be taken seriously.The bottoming-out of web traffic quickly made Swift coverage a reliable banker for online publications, and amid the frothy celebrity-angled coverage, any negative reviews made targets of their authors. This reached fever pitch when Pitchfork gave Swift’s 2020 album Folklore a very high 8.0, which nonetheless brought down the record’s otherwise perfect score on reviews aggregator Metacritic. The critic got doxed, creating a culture of fear that prompted US publication Paste to publish their spicily negative review of 2024’s The Tortured Poets Department with no byline, citing concern for the writer’s safety.Recently, some high-profile critics have declared all this has made criticism toothless. Frankly, I don’t know what they’re reading – and speaking from personal experience, I think the Swifties have calmed down – but the idea has caught on: that once critics wouldn’t write about her, but now they can’t.LSImage credits: Erika Goldring/TAS24/Getty Images for TAS Rights ManagementShe made pop star style normieView image in fullscreenIn the celebrity world where A-listers are constantly trying to outdo each other with headline-grabbing outfits and museum-worthy archival pulls, Swift’s style is an anomaly. Aside from a few memorable blunders (a“This is my fight song” teepaired with leather harness; theSesame Street paletteof ME!), her style is remarkable for its sheer inoffensiveness and apparent lack of consistent taste, as well as her ability to make the most expensive couture looks feel as if they were bought at the mall. Has she ever truly slayed? It’s debatable. That’s precisely what fascinates Lauren Sherman, a Puck fashion reporter.OMYou once referred to Taylor Swift’s style as“Anthropologiegirlgonewild”. That felt like a fashion risk without leaving her comfort zone.Image Credits: Jason Howard/Bauer-Griffin/GC ImagesShe thrived on negativityView image in fullscreenOther than maybe Drake, no pop star has got richer off making lemons into lemonade. When critics said the teenage Swift didn’t write her own songs, she wrote 2010’s sublime Speak Now entirely alone. Ye invading her MTV awards acceptance speech made her a household name. The media ragging on her for having too many boyfriends gave us Blank Space. Losing out to West in the public relations game inspiredReputation, her best album. Her industry nemesis buying the rights to her first six albums inspired the Taylor’s Versions coup de grace, and in turn the Eras tour, which grossed more than $1bn – affording her to buy the masters herself. I would love to know whether she’s ultimately grateful for any of these misfortunes. At any rate, last year she said it outright when critics gave her album The Life of a Showgirlmiddling reviews: “The rule of show business is if it’s the first week of my album release, and you are saying either my name or my album title, you’re helping.” More recently, she revealed that she tells young songwriters that criticism is a gift: “Make art about it.”LSImage Credits: Kevin Winter/TAS24/Getty Images for TAS Rights Management; Christopher Polk/Getty ImagesShe made her publicist a starView image in fullscreenTree Paine and Swift.In October 2023, Swift was on a red carpet for the premiere of The Eras Tour film. She’ll tell me what to do.”The idea that any one person could truly control pop’s ultimate Machiavelli feels faintly ludicrous. But Paine is the bad cop who lets her employer play at being sunshine and rainbows.
